title: EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) - Transport Provisions
summary: The CSRD, which became fully applicable in 2024, requires large companies to report detailed sustainability information including Scope 3 emissions from logistics and transportation services. The directive mandates standardized reporting on environmental impacts across supply chains, with specific requirements for freight and logistics service providers. Companies must now report on their transportation-related carbon footprint, sustainable fuel usage, and modal shift initiatives using standardized European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS).
why_matters: Freight forwarders serving European clients or subsidiaries must now provide detailed sustainability data to support their customers' CSRD compliance. This creates new service opportunities but also compliance costs, as forwarders need robust carbon tracking systems, modal analysis capabilities, and standardized reporting formats. Client relationships increasingly depend on the ability to provide granular emissions data and sustainability metrics.
full_brief:
# EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — Transport Provisions

**Regulatory Fact Document | MONITORING | Regenerated 2026-06-03**

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document provides a workspace-anchored regulatory fact document covering the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (EU) 2022/2464 (CSRD) and its operational consequences for workspaces active in freight forwarding, transport, and logistics supply chain roles. The document integrates the original CSRD text, the April 2025 "Stop-the-Clock" Directive (EU) 2025/794, and the February 2026 Omnibus I Directive (EU) 2026/470, which together constitute the current operative legal landscape as of June 2026.

**Convention notes:**
- All binding-obligation language is either (a) a verbatim span from a source block with an inline citation, (b) prefixed with a labeled inference token, or (c) routed to a *Legal Confirmation Required:* callout.
- "The workspace" refers to the freight forwarding or logistics service provider operation described in the workspace profile. No individual or company name is used.
- Dates stated as facts are cited to a source block. Dates stated as estimates are labeled "Analytical inference."
- The regulatory landscape is actively evolving. A re-check window is set at the end of this document.
- The four required slots for regulation-family items are covered explicitly: effective date, primary deadline, jurisdictional scope, and penalty/enforcement provision.

*Source: Directive (EU) 2022/2464, European Parliament and Council, 14 December 2022.* https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32022L2464

---

# What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

## What the CSRD Is

The CSRD is a directive of the European Parliament and Council of the EU published on 16 December 2022. FACT: The directive was published in "OJ L 322, 16.12.2022, pp. 15–80." *Source: Directive (EU) 2022/2464, EUR-Lex, 16 December 2022.* https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32022L2464

FACT: The CSRD amends "Regulation (EU) No 537/2014, Directive 2004/109/EC, Directive 2006/43/EC and Directive 2013/34/EU, as regards corporate sustainability reporting." *Source: Directive (EU) 2022/2464, EUR-Lex, 16 December 2022.* https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32022L2464

In plain terms: the CSRD replaces the Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and extends mandatory, audited sustainability reporting to a significantly wider set of companies. FACT: "CSRD turns sustainability reporting into audited, standardized reporting (ESRS). It becomes part of annual management reporting and must be verified by accredited auditors." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

FACT: "ESRS are data-intensive disclosures covering greenhouse gas emissions, energy, waste, water, recycling, and social metrics." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

## Effective Date

**Required slot — effective date:** FACT: The original CSRD was published in the Official Journal on 16 December 2022. *Source: European Commission corporate sustainability reporting policy timeline, European Commission, 14 December 2022.* https://finance.ec.europa.eu/capital-markets-union-and-financial-markets/company-reporting-and-auditing/company-reporting/corporate-sustainability-reporting_en

FACT: As amended by the Omnibus I Directive, the current consolidated version entered force on 18 March 2026. The Omnibus I Directive "(Directive (EU) 2026/470) was published in the Official Journal of the European Union on 26 February 2026" and "will come into force in 20 days" of publication, placing entry into force on 18 March 2026. *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 16 December 2025 (updated 26 February 2026).* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

## Jurisdictional Scope

**Required slot — jurisdictional scope:** FACT: The regulation applies to "large undertakings, and small and medium-sized undertakings, except micro undertakings, which are public-interest entities." Under the Omnibus I Directive, the scope narrows to "EU entities or groups with more than 1,000 employees and more than €450 million net turnover." FACT: Non-EU entities are in scope when they have "consolidated turnover exceeding €450 million in the EU" and "a subsidiary or a branch in the EU with turnover exceeding €200 million." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 16 December 2025 (updated 26 February 2026).* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

## Primary Compliance Deadline

**Required slot — primary deadline:** FACT: Under the Omnibus I Directive, EU entities meeting the revised thresholds "will have reporting obligations for financial years beginning on or after January 1, 2027 (reporting in 2028)." *Source: Deloitte Heads Up — European Sustainability Reporting Omnibus, Deloitte, 14 January 2026.* https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2026/eu-sustainability-reporting-omnibus-esrs-updates

Non-EU parent entities at enterprise level will have reporting obligations for "financial years beginning on or after January 1, 2028 (reporting in 2029)." *Source: Deloitte Heads Up — European Sustainability Reporting Omnibus, Deloitte, 14 January 2026.* https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2026/eu-sustainability-reporting-omnibus-esrs-updates

## Why It Applies to the Workspace

FACT: "The transport and logistics sector is one of the most directly affected industries under the CSRD framework. Accounting for approximately 25% of the EU's total greenhouse gas emissions, the sector faces intense scrutiny from regulators, investors, and customers demanding verifiable sustainability data." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

For the workspace in its capacity as a freight forwarder:

**Direct applicability:** *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace itself crosses the >1,000 employees and >€450 million net turnover thresholds that trigger mandatory CSRD reporting under the Omnibus I Directive requires case-by-case determination against current consolidated financials and head count.

**Indirect applicability (Scope 3 pull-through):** FACT: "CSRD directly impacts listed and large-sized companies operating in the EU and indirectly impacts their entire supply chain, particularly for the reporting of scope 3 emissions." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

Operational implication: Cargo-owner clients of the workspace who are themselves in CSRD scope will generate demand for shipment-level emissions data from the workspace regardless of whether the workspace is directly in scope. This demand arises from the Scope 3 reporting obligation that attaches to the cargo owner's own CSRD disclosure. FACT: "A logistics company that cannot provide this data risks losing contracts to competitors who can." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

---

# Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### MONITORING — Scope determination: direct vs. indirect applicability

**Action:** Conduct a threshold assessment against the Omnibus I revised criteria (>1,000 employees AND >€450 million net annual turnover on a consolidated basis) to determine whether the workspace falls within the direct CSRD reporting scope for financial year 2027 (first report due 2028).

**Why now:** Member states have until 19 March 2027 to transpose the Omnibus I Directive. FACT: "Member states are required to transpose the directive's provisions related to the CSRD by March 19, 2027." *Source: Deloitte Heads Up — European Sustainability Reporting Omnibus, Deloitte, 14 January 2026.* https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2026/eu-sustainability-reporting-omnibus-esrs-updates

Analytical inference: Transposition will introduce national-level variation in exact threshold application and penalty calibration. Workspaces that have not confirmed in-scope or out-of-scope status before transposition risk being caught by national implementing legislation without having built data infrastructure.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether subsidiary structures, group consolidation rules, or non-EU parent configurations affect the workspace's threshold calculation requires counsel review against Articles 19a, 29a, and 40a of the directive as amended by Omnibus I.

---

### COMPETITIVE EDGE — Emissions data readiness as contract-retention tool

**Action:** Establish shipment-level CO₂e data collection and reporting capability now, irrespective of direct regulatory scope, to retain cargo-owner clients who are themselves in wave-1 CSRD scope and need Scope 3 data for FY 2024 and FY 2025 reports (already due or being prepared).

**Why now:** Wave-1 companies (large public-interest entities formerly under NFRD) are already reporting. FACT: "The first companies subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) have to apply the new rules for the first time in the 2024 financial year, for reports published in 2025." *Source: European Commission corporate sustainability reporting policy page, European Commission, 9 December 2025.* https://finance.ec.europa.eu/capital-markets-union-and-financial-markets/company-reporting-and-auditing/company-reporting/corporate-sustainability-reporting_en

Operational implication: Cargo owners currently publishing 2024 sustainability reports are active today, and their Scope 3 data requests to the workspace are live, not future obligations.

---

### MONITORING — Simplified ESRS Delegated Act: watch for publication

**Action:** Monitor EFRAG and European Commission for publication of the simplified ESRS Delegated Act, expected by mid-2026, which will set the exact datapoint requirements applicable for FY 2027 reporting. Do not build reporting infrastructure against the current full ESRS until the simplified standards are confirmed.

**Why now:** FACT: "The new standards are expected to apply from FY 2027 onwards, both for wave 1 companies that remain in scope and for wave 2 companies entering CSRD reporting for the first time." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

Building infrastructure against the full ESRS datapoint set before simplification is confirmed risks over-investment in reporting obligations that may be reduced.

---

### COST ALERT — EU ETS extension to transport: intersecting cost pressure

**Action:** Factor the EU ETS road transport extension (scheduled for 2027) into cost modeling alongside CSRD compliance investment. FACT: "With the new CO₂-toll for trucks (Germany) and extension of the EU ETS in 2027, emission costs become a tangible aspect of transportation services." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

Operational implication: The EU ETS extension means that CSRD emissions data infrastructure built for reporting purposes will simultaneously serve the commercial need to price and pass through carbon costs — reinforcing the return on investment of building that infrastructure now.

---

# How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

## Direct Reporting Entity Role

FACT: Under the Omnibus I Directive, the revised scope applies to "EU entities or groups with more than 1,000 employees and more than €450 million net turnover." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace meets both thresholds on a standalone or consolidated basis, and whether any group structure creates enterprise-level reporting obligations under Article 40a, requires counsel and finance review.

## Value Chain / Scope 3 Supplier Role

Regardless of direct-scope status, the workspace sits within the Scope 3 reporting boundary of cargo-owner clients. FACT: "GHG emissions of the entire supply chain must be reported, including scope 3 emissions. In a company's total carbon footprint, scope 3 emissions constitute the largest portion, for which suppliers are responsible." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

Operational implication: For workspaces acting as freight forwarders and third-party logistics providers to cargo owners in live entertainment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production, and humanitarian supply chains — all sectors where clients are likely large enterprises — Scope 3 emissions data requests are a current commercial reality, not a future possibility.

## Value Chain Cap (Omnibus I Protection)

FACT: Under the Omnibus I Directive, "limits requests to value chain entities with up to 1,000 employees to the requirements of the future VSME Delegated Act." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

Analytical inference: If the workspace has fewer than 1,000 employees, the value chain cap provides a formal protection limiting what directly-in-scope clients can contractually demand, capping requests at the VSME standard once that standard is adopted. The VSME Delegated Act is expected in June 2026 (see Section 6 below).

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The operative force of the value chain cap depends on the transposing national legislation in each member state where the workspace's clients are domiciled. Whether this protection is immediately enforceable against a client contract clause requires counsel review.

---

# Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

*This section covers the primary CSRD text (Directive (EU) 2022/2464 as amended) and the Omnibus I Directive (EU) 2026/470 as they apply to transport and logistics operations.*

## Article 19a — Sustainability Reporting Obligation

FACT: "Large undertakings, and small and medium-sized undertakings, except micro undertakings, which are public-interest entities as defined in point (a) of point (1) of Article 2 shall include in the management report information necessary to understand the undertaking's impacts on sustainability matters, and information necessary to understand how sustainability matters affect the undertaking's development, performance and position." *Source: Directive (EU) 2022/2464 consolidated text, EUR-Lex, 17 April 2025.* https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/dir/2022/2464/2025-04-17/eng

Operational implication: For a workspace in scope, the sustainability report is not a standalone disclosure — it is part of the management report, the same document used for financial reporting purposes, subject to the same governance and audit requirements.

## Double Materiality Assessment

FACT: "A core element of CSRD is the so-called double materiality assessment which defines which specific sections a company needs to report on." The two types are: "(1) outside-in, financial materiality: when a sustainability risk affects a company's financial performance, cash flow, its access to finance, or cost of capital" and "(2) inside-out, environmental, and social materiality: how a company impacts the environment and social spheres." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

Operational implication: For a freight forwarder, the double materiality assessment is unlikely to result in climate change being assessed as non-material. FACT: "Since pretty much any company that is operating assets is generating GHG emissions (either directly or indirectly through its supply chain), the climate change section of CSRD concerns the majority of companies." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

## ESRS Standards Applicable to Transport

FACT: Transport and logistics companies subject to CSRD must address the following ESRS topics: "climate change (ESRS E1), pollution (ESRS E2), and workers in the value chain (ESRS S2) are almost always material." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

FACT: "ESRS E1 (Climate change) retains its special status. While data points within ESRS E1 have been reduced, it remains the most substantive topical standard. It is the only environmental standard that keeps its own dedicated anticipated financial effects requirement (E1-11), due to the need to distinguish between physical and transition risks. For E2–E5, these disclosures have been centralised into ESRS 2 (SBM-3)." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

## Assurance Requirements

FACT: Under the Omnibus I Directive, the reference to an EU reasonable assurance standard is removed. The directive "Removes the reference to an EU reasonable assurance standard. EC to adopt an EU limited assurance standard by July 2027." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

## Sector-Specific Standards: Removed from Mandate

FACT: The Omnibus I Directive "Removes requirement to issue sector-based standards. The EC will be able to provide sector-specific guidance if there is demand from entities subject to CSRD in a specific sector." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

Operational implication: The transport-sector-specific ESRS that were anticipated under the original CSRD are no longer mandated. Any sector guidance will now be voluntary, and its timeline is unconfirmed. Workspaces planning reporting architecture should use the general ESRS framework rather than waiting for a transport-specific standard.

FACT: The original CSRD source noted "For specific sectors, including transport, a dedicated set of reporting ESRS will be issued in coming years." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics — this provision has been superseded by the Omnibus I removal of the sector-standard mandate. No transport ESRS is now scheduled as a binding instrument.

## Voluntary Reporting Standard (VSME)

FACT: "Entities that are not subject to the scope of the CSRD may choose to voluntarily report in accordance with the Voluntary Sustainability Standard for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (VSME)." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

FACT: "The VSME provides a framework for companies outside mandatory scope. It includes two tiers: 1) a Basic Module covering core environmental, social, and governance metrics, and 2) a Comprehensive Module for companies that want to go further." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

---

# Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

## 1. MONITORING — Simplified ESRS Delegated Act (EC, expected mid-2026)

**Type:** Delegated act amending the European Sustainability Reporting Standards.
**Issuing body:** European Commission, based on EFRAG technical advice.
**Status and phase:** EFRAG submitted its simplified ESRS technical advice to the European Commission in December 2025. FACT: "EFRAG turns over simplified ESRS technical advice to the European Commission (04 December 2025)." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

FACT: "The Simplified ESRS Delegated Act [is] expected by mid-2026." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

**Impact on workspace:** The simplified ESRS will set the exact datapoint obligations for FY 2027 reporting (first report 2028). Until adopted, the full current ESRS applies to wave-1 entities, and wave-2 entities cannot build compliant reporting against a finalized standard. FACT: "EFRAG's amended ESRS reduces mandatory datapoints by 61% and 71% overall when voluntary items were removed." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

Re-check window: Q3 2026. If the Delegated Act is not published by September 2026, the workspace should escalate to legal and operations for contingency planning.

---

## 2. MONITORING — VSME Delegated Act (EC, expected June 2026)

**Type:** Delegated act formalizing the Voluntary Sustainability Standard for SMEs.
**Issuing body:** European Commission.
**Status:** FACT: "The European Commission is expected to publish a delegated act containing the VSME standard in June 2026, which will be based on the current European Commission recommendation." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

**Impact on workspace:** The VSME, once adopted, establishes the ceiling for what in-scope clients can formally request from value chain partners with fewer than 1,000 employees. It also establishes the voluntary baseline that sub-threshold workspaces can use to respond to client ESG questionnaires.

Re-check window: July 2026 — confirm whether the Delegated Act was published in June 2026 as projected.

---

## 3. MONITORING — Member State Transposition of Omnibus I (deadline 19 March 2027)

**Type:** National transposition of Directive (EU) 2026/470.
**Issuing body:** EU Member States individually.
**Deadline:** FACT: "Member states are required to transpose the directive's provisions related to the CSRD by March 19, 2027." *Source: Deloitte Heads Up — European Sustainability Reporting Omnibus, Deloitte, 14 January 2026.* https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2026/eu-sustainability-reporting-omnibus-esrs-updates

**Impact on workspace:** The operative penalty and enforcement framework, and the exact threshold calibrations at national level, will not be finalized until transposition. Workspaces operating across multiple EU member states face the possibility of divergent national implementations during the transposition window.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Which member state's implementation governs for a workspace with operations in multiple EU countries requires counsel determination.

---

## 4. MONITORING — EU ETS Road Transport Extension (scheduled 2027)

**Type:** Regulatory implementation — EU Emissions Trading System.
**Issuing body:** European Commission.
**Status:** FACT: "Extension of the EU ETS in 2027, emission costs become a tangible aspect of transportation services." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

**Impact on workspace:** The CSRD emissions data infrastructure built for Scope 1/2/3 reporting will simultaneously serve cost management for EU ETS compliance. This creates a dual-purpose return on investment for emissions data systems. However, road transport operations will face direct carbon cost exposure when ETS 2 takes effect.

Re-check window: Q4 2026.

---

## 5. MONITORING — CSRD Scope Review (April 2031)

**Type:** Legislative review.
**Issuing body:** European Commission, reporting to Parliament and Council.
**Status:** FACT: "The 'Omnibus' directive also requires the European Commission to submit a report to the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union by April 2031 and every three years thereafter assessing whether and how the scope of the CSRD should be extended." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

Analytical inference: Workspaces that currently fall below the revised thresholds should treat the 2031 review as a horizon date. Companies that "maintain reporting capabilities today avoid a costly restart tomorrow." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

---

# Threshold Questions

## Decided: Transport Sector Is Not Exempt

The CSRD does not provide a sector exemption for transport or logistics. FACT: "The transport and logistics sector is one of the most directly affected industries under the CSRD framework." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

## Decided: Double Materiality Assessment Is Not Optional

FACT: "The DMA remains the starting point for determining which topics a company must report on, and the fundamental dual-lens approach, assessing both impact materiality and financial materiality, is preserved." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

## Decided: Sector-Specific Transport ESRS Is Not Coming (Binding Form)

The Omnibus I Directive removed the European Commission's obligation to issue binding sector standards. FACT: "Removes requirement to issue sector-based standards. The EC will be able to provide sector-specific guidance if there is demand." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

## Decided: Listed SMEs Excluded from Mandatory Scope

FACT: Under the Omnibus I Directive, the revised scope "Excludes listed SMEs." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

## *Legal Confirmation Required*: Direct Scope Status for the Workspace

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in its current consolidated structure, exceeds both the >1,000 employee and >€450 million net turnover thresholds as of balance sheet date for FY 2027, and whether any group structure triggers enterprise-level obligations under Article 40a, requires quantitative financial and legal analysis specific to the workspace.

## *Legal Confirmation Required*: Wave-1 Exemption Window

FACT: "The 'Omnibus' directive permits Member States to exempt entities with less than €450 million in net turnover and fewer than 1,000 employees (on a consolidated basis where applicable) from reporting obligations for financial years starting between 1 January 2025 and 31 December 2026. The applicability of this provision will have to be clarified by each Member State." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace qualifies for this exemption window, and whether the member state(s) where it is domiciled have exercised this option, requires counsel and finance review.

## *Legal Confirmation Required*: Subsidiary Exemption Availability

FACT: "The 'Omnibus' directive extends this [subsidiary] exemption to all public interest entities listed on EU-regulated markets." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace's corporate structure enables use of the subsidiary exemption through a parent entity's consolidated ESRS report requires counsel review.

---

# Substantive Requirements

## 8.1 Greenhouse Gas Emissions Reporting (ESRS E1)

This is the most operationally demanding requirement for the workspace.

**Scope 1 (direct emissions):** FACT: Transport companies must "Disclose Scope 1 emissions (owned fleet fuel consumption)." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

**Scope 2 (indirect energy):** FACT: "Scope 2 emissions (electricity for warehouses, terminals, and charging infrastructure)" must be disclosed. *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

**Scope 3 (value chain):** FACT: "Scope 3 emissions (subcontracted transport, business travel, upstream fuel and energy-related activities)" must be disclosed. *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

Operational implication: For a freight forwarder operating primarily as an asset-light intermediary — subcontracting air, road, and ocean carriage — Scope 3 emissions from subcontracted carriers are the dominant emissions category. FACT: "Scope 3 emissions from subcontracted transport are typically one of the largest emission categories for logistics companies." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

**ESRS E1 special status:** FACT: "ESRS E1 (Climate change) retains its special status" and "is the only environmental standard that keeps its own dedicated anticipated financial effects requirement (E1-11), due to the need to distinguish between physical and transition risks." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

## 8.2 Transition Plans

FACT: Companies subject to CSRD must "Provide a credible decarbonisation plan aligned with the Paris Agreement's 1.5-degree target. This includes fleet electrification timelines, alternative fuel adoption strategies, and modal shift targets (e.g., road-to-rail or road-to-inland waterway)." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

Operational implication: For workspaces with road operations, the transition plan will require publicly disclosed modal shift and electrification timelines, with capital expenditure attached. These plans become externally audited commitments, not internal targets.

## 8.3 Workforce and Social Disclosures (ESRS S1/S2)

FACT: Companies must "Report on driver working conditions, health and safety records, training programmes, and fair wage practices. For logistics companies relying on subcontracted drivers, disclosures must extend to the value chain workforce." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

## 8.4 Governance and Due Diligence

FACT: Companies must "Demonstrate that sustainability risks are integrated into board-level decision-making, with clear accountability structures and due diligence processes covering the entire supply chain." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

## 8.5 Digital Tagging and Report Format

FACT: "All CSRD reports must be prepared in a digital, machine-readable format (XHTML with XBRL tagging) and subject to limited assurance by an independent auditor." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

## 8.6 Value Chain Data Collection

FACT: Companies must "Establish systems to collect sustainability data from suppliers, subcontractors, and customers. This is particularly demanding for logistics companies with fragmented subcontractor networks." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

Operational implication: For workspaces that book air cargo via multiple airline IATA codes and ocean freight via multiple vessel operators, the subcontractor data collection requirement maps directly onto carrier-level primary data acquisition. Generic spend-based Scope 3 estimates will not satisfy the "best available data" standard under ESRS.

---

*Section 9 — Product-Specific Compliance Status: No content for this section as of June 2026. The workspace does not manufacture or sell physical products under the workspace brand within the scope of CSRD product-level disclosure requirements. This section is inapplicable.*

---

# Registration and Reporting Obligations

## Primary Registration

There is no standalone registration regime for CSRD (unlike EPR producer registration regimes). FACT: The CSRD inserts sustainability reporting into the management report structure under Directive 2013/34/EU. The workspace's primary obligation is to incorporate sustainability information "in the management report," which is filed in the ordinary course of statutory financial reporting. *Source: Directive (EU) 2022/2464, EUR-Lex, 16 December 2022.* https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32022L2464

## Assurance Engagement

FACT: Reports must be "verified by accredited auditors in line with European sustainability reporting standards (ESRS)." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

For the current transitional period, the standard is limited assurance. FACT: The Omnibus I Directive removes the reference to a reasonable assurance standard and requires the EC to "adopt an EU limited assurance standard by July 2027." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

## Scope 3 Data Request Obligations Toward the Workspace

FACT: Under the Omnibus I value chain cap, "Limits requests to value chain entities with up to 1,000 employees to the requirements of the future VSME Delegated Act." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

The VSME Delegated Act is not yet published (expected June 2026). Until published, there is no formal cap on what clients can request. Analytical inference: In the interim, clients may issue bespoke data requests that exceed what the VSME will ultimately require. Workspaces should negotiate data-sharing templates that track toward VSME scope rather than accepting open-ended ad hoc requests.

## Methodology for Emissions Data

FACT: "The ESRS expects companies to use the best available data, starting with primary data from subcontractors where possible and falling back to industry-average emission factors where it is not. Practically, this means establishing data-sharing agreements with your most significant subcontractors and using recognised methodologies such as the GLEC Framework (Global Logistics Emissions Council) to calculate emissions per shipment or tonne-kilometre." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

---

# Operational System Requirements

## System 1: Emissions Data Infrastructure (Scope 1/2/3)

**Scope:** The workspace needs to capture fuel consumption per vehicle and route, electricity use per facility, and subcontractor-level carrier emissions per shipment. FACT: Systems should "Implement systems to capture fuel consumption per vehicle and route, energy use per facility, emissions factors for different transport modes, and social KPIs such as accident rates and driver turnover." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

**Gap from current operational baseline:** For workspaces managing cargo operations without integrated shipment-level emissions tracking, the gap between current state and the requirement is foundational. Telematics, TMS integration, and carrier data-sharing agreements must all be established before FY 2027 data collection begins.

**Deadline:** FY 2027 is the first reporting year for wave-2 entities under the Omnibus I schedule. Data collection for that year begins 1 January 2027. Systems must be operational before that date.

**Recommended reference methodology:** FACT: The GLEC Framework is identified as a "recognised methodology" for calculating emissions per shipment or tonne-kilometre. *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

ISO 14083 (standard for quantification and reporting of GHG emissions from transport chain operations) is a relevant adjacent standard. *Specific details on ISO 14083 integration requirements are not available from the provided source blocks as of June 2026; see New Sources Identified.*

## System 2: Double Materiality Assessment Process

**Scope:** A formal, board-validated DMA identifying material sustainability topics for the workspace's specific operations.

**Gap from current operational baseline:** *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any materiality assessment conducted under prior NFRD compliance (if applicable) meets the CSRD DMA standard requires counsel and sustainability advisor review.

FACT: "Engage stakeholders including drivers, warehouse workers, customers, regulators, and local communities. Score each topic on both financial and impact dimensions, document thresholds, and have the results validated by your board." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

## System 3: Subcontractor Data-Sharing Agreements

**Scope:** Standard contractual provisions with key carrier subcontractors — airline operators (for air-primary routes), road carriers, and ocean carriers — requiring shipment-level emissions data in a format compatible with ESRS and GLEC methodology.

**Gap from current operational baseline:** Most carrier contracts do not currently include emissions data delivery obligations. These clauses must be introduced at contract renewal or renegotiation.

Operational implication: For workspaces with air-primary transport operations where short-notice charter or spot-market bookings are common (e.g., live events, humanitarian cargo), standardized emissions data flows from carriers may require system integration agreements rather than purely contractual obligations.

## System 4: Digital Report Preparation (XBRL/ESEF)

**Scope:** FACT: Reports must be in "XHTML with inline XBRL tagging as required by the European Single Electronic Format (ESEF)." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

**Gap:** If the workspace currently prepares sustainability disclosures in PDF or narrative-only formats, an XBRL-tagged digital reporting system must be adopted before the first CSRD-compliant report is due.

---

# Exemptions and Edge Cases

## Wave-1 Entity Exemption Window (Member State Option)

FACT: "The 'Omnibus' directive permits Member States to exempt entities with less than €450 million in net turnover and fewer than 1,000 employees (on a consolidated basis where applicable) from reporting obligations for financial years starting between 1 January 2025 and 31 December 2026. The applicability of this provision will have to be clarified by each Member State." *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

Operational implication: Workspaces that are wave-1 entities (large public-interest entities formerly under NFRD) but will fall below the Omnibus I thresholds should confirm with counsel whether their member state has exercised this opt-out for FY 2025 and FY 2026.

## Subsidiary Exemption

FACT: "The 'Omnibus' directive extends this [subsidiary] exemption to all public interest entities listed on EU-regulated markets," allowing subsidiaries included in a parent's CSRD-compliant ESRS report to satisfy their own sustainability reporting requirement. *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Eligibility for the subsidiary exemption depends on the parent entity's reporting status and whether the parent's report includes the subsidiary's material information. Counsel review is required.

## Non-EU Entities

FACT: Under Omnibus I, non-EU entities are in scope when the group "generates net turnover in the European Union exceeding €450 million at the group level for each of the last two consecutive financial years" and "has at least one EU subsidiary or branch that generated more than €200 million in net turnover in the preceding financial year." Reporting obligations for such entities apply "for financial years beginning on or after January 1, 2028 (reporting in 2029)." *Source: Deloitte Heads Up — European Sustainability Reporting Omnibus, Deloitte, 14 January 2026.* https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2026/eu-sustainability-reporting-omnibus-esrs-updates

*Legal Confirmation Required:* For workspaces structured as non-EU parent entities with EU subsidiaries or branches, the enterprise-level reporting obligation under Article 40a is distinct from entity-level obligations under Articles 19a and 29a; whether satisfying one satisfies the other requires counsel review.

## Listed SMEs — Excluded

FACT: The Omnibus I Directive "Excludes listed SMEs" from the mandatory scope. *Source: PwC 'Omnibus' directive finalised, PwC, 26 February 2026.* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html

FACT: Listed SMEs "Removed from mandatory CSRD scope. May report voluntarily using the simplified Voluntary Sustainability Reporting Standard for SMEs (VSME) standard." *Source: ClimatePartner — CSRD after the Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026.* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

---

# Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

## Competitive Data Positioning in Tender Processes

FACT: "In upcoming tender processes, having transparent and reliable emissions data may become one of the key factors alongside the service price that sets suppliers apart and gives them a competitive advantage." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

Analytical inference: For workspaces competing for contracts from cargo owners in luxury goods, live events, and film and TV production — sectors where clients are disproportionately large enterprises within CSRD scope — emissions data provision is transitioning from differentiator to baseline expectation. Competitors who can demonstrate shipment-level CO₂e reporting to ESRS standards at tender stage will displace those who cannot.

## GLEC Framework as Methodology Alternative

The GLEC Framework (Global Logistics Emissions Council) is the industry-consensus methodology for logistics emissions calculation. FACT: The Plan Be Eco source identifies the GLEC Framework as a "recognised methodology" for calculating emissions per shipment or tonne-kilometre under ESRS. *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

Analytical inference: The GLEC Framework (now reflected in ISO 14083) provides a methodology the workspace can deploy for both internal calculation and client-facing data delivery. Its use positions workspace outputs as methodology-consistent with what in-scope clients will need for their own ESRS E1 filings.

## Book-and-Claim as an Interim Bridging Mechanism

Analytical inference: For workspaces that cannot source primary carrier emissions data for all transport legs — particularly in air freight where multiple charter operators or GSSA arrangements may be involved — book-and-claim mechanisms for SAF or green freight certificates may provide an interim means of demonstrating emission reduction effort for client CSRD reporting purposes, pending development of full primary-data supply chains. This is an emerging area; authoritative ESRS guidance on book-and-claim acceptability for Scope 3 accounting has not been confirmed in the provided source blocks as of June 2026.

| 1 January 2024 | Wave-1 reporting period begins (FY 2024 data) | In force | Wave-1 clients already reporting; Scope 3 data requests live |
| 2025 (published) | Wave-1 first CSRD reports published | In force | Client data requests active now |
| 16 April 2025 | Stop-the-Clock Directive (EU) 2025/794 published in Official Journal | In force as of 17 April 2025 | Wave-2 start delayed by two years |
| 17 April 2025 | Stop-the-Clock Directive enters into force | In force | Wave-2 reporting for FY 2025 postponed to FY 2027 |
| 31 December 2025 | Member state transposition deadline for Stop-the-Clock | Past deadline | Monitor national implementation status |
| 11 July 2025 | "Quick fix" ESRS amendments adopted by EC | In force from 13 November 2025 | Wave-1 companies: reduced additional datapoints for FY 2025–2026 |
| 26 February 2026 | Omnibus I Directive (EU) 2026/470 published in Official Journal | In force | Revised scope (>1,000 employees AND >€450M turnover) confirmed |
| 18 March 2026 | Omnibus I Directive enters into force | In force | Current operative scope in effect |
| June 2026 (projected) | VSME Delegated Act expected publication | Pending | Monitor: establishes value-chain cap ceiling and voluntary baseline |
| Mid-2026 (projected) | Simplified ESRS Delegated Act expected publication | Pending | Do not finalize FY 2027 reporting architecture until confirmed |
| 19 March 2027 | Member state transposition deadline for Omnibus I CSRD provisions | Future | National penalty and enforcement framework crystallizes |
| 1 January 2027 | Wave-2 reporting period begins (FY 2027 data collection) | Future — ACTION DATE | Data systems must be operational; scope confirmed under national law |
| 2028 (report publication) | Wave-2 first CSRD reports due | Future | First public disclosure for entities crossing Omnibus I thresholds |
| January 2028 | Non-EU enterprise-level (Article 40a) reporting period begins | Future | Relevant for workspaces with non-EU parent structures |
| April 2031 | EC mandatory review of CSRD scope | Future | Scope may expand; maintains data readiness as strategic hedge |

*Source for timeline facts: Directive (EU) 2022/2464, EUR-Lex, 16 December 2022* https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32022L2464; *Deloitte Heads Up, Deloitte, 14 January 2026* https://dart.deloitte.com/USDART/home/publications/deloitte/heads-up/2026/eu-sustainability-reporting-omnibus-esrs-updates; *PwC Omnibus In Brief, PwC, 26 February 2026* https://viewpoint.pwc.com/gx/en/pwc/in-briefs/ib_int202527.html; *De Brauw Stop-the-Clock, De Brauw, 23 April 2025* https://www.debrauw.com/articles/omnibus-stop-the-clock-amendments-to-csrd-and-csddd-now-final; *ClimatePartner CSRD after Omnibus, ClimatePartner, 14 April 2026* https://www.climatepartner.com/en/blog/csrd-after-the-omnibus

---

## **Penalty / Enforcement**

**Required slot — penalty summary:**

FACT: "Penalties for non-compliance are to be established by the EU member states. Following the Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) of which CSRD is the successor, companies may face fines of up to either €10 million, 5% of the total annual turnover or twice the total profits made/losses avoided due to the breach." *Source: Shipzero CSRD Logistics Guide, Shipzero, 26 October 2023.* https://www.shipzero.com/blog/csrds-ripple-effect-on-transport-and-logistics

FACT: Non-compliance also carries "significant commercial risk: major shippers and retailers are increasingly requiring CSRD-aligned sustainability data from their logistics providers as a condition of doing business." *Source: Plan Be Eco — CSRD/DMA for Transport & Logistics, Plan Be Eco, 7 April 2026.* https://planbe.eco/en/blog/csrd-for-the-transport-and-logistics-industry

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The exact penalty regime applicable to the workspace will depend on the transposing national legislation in the member state(s) of incorporation. Until transposition is complete (deadline 19 March 2027), the national enforcement framework is not finalized.
